eviction.court_process_summary_rule:
{"statutes":"Va. Code §§ 55.1-1245, 55.1-1245 (two versions: current and effective July 1, 2028)","notice_requirements":{"nonpayment_of_rent":"5-day written notice to pay or vacate (current version); same with extended notice periods (2028 version)","holdover_tenant":"30-day notice to terminate for month-to-month tenancy","lease_violation":"21-day notice to cure for material breach; 30-day notice before termination","no_cause":"30-day notice for month-to-month (VRLTA-covered units)","criminal_activity":"Immediate termination allowed for drug crimes or criminal acts threatening health/safety"},"full_text":"§ 55.1-1245. Noncompliance with rental agreement; monetary penalty.
A. Material breach. If the tenant commits a material breach of the rental agreement or a violation of § 55.1-1227 that materially affects health and safety, the landlord may give written notice specifying the tenant's acts or omissions and stating that the rental agreement will terminate no less than 30 days after receipt of the notice if the breach is not remedied within 21 days.
B. If the breach is remediable by repair or the payment of damages and the tenant adequately remedies the breach within 21 days, the rental agreement does not terminate.
C. Nonremediable breach. If the breach is nonremediable by repair or the payment of damages, the landlord may give written notice stating that the rental agreement will terminate no less than 30 days after receipt.
D. Immediate termination. If the tenant's breach consists of or constitutes a criminal or intentional act that is nonremediable and threatens health or safety, including drug-related criminal activity (as defined under § 54.1-3400), the landlord may terminate immediately and bring an action for possession. Court must hold first hearing within 15 calendar days of service.
E. Repeated breach. If a tenant who has previously received a written notice to remedy a breach and has remedied it commits the same type of breach again, the landlord may give 30 days' written notice to terminate.
F. Nonpayment of rent. If the tenant fails to pay rent when due, the landlord may give written notice requiring the tenant to pay rent or vacate within 5 days. For NSF checks, electronic payment failures, or bad faith stop payments, the tenant must pay within 5 days by cash, certified check, cashier's check, or completed electronic transfer.
J. COVID-19 Rental History Protection (2025). Landlords with more than 4 units may not take adverse action based solely on payment history or eviction records from March 12, 2020 through 30 days after the state of emergency ends. Applicants may dispute within 7 days. Violation carries $1,000 statutory damages plus attorney fees."}

{"statutes":"Va. Code § 55.1-1226","full_text":"§ 55.1-1226. Security deposits.
A. Maximum deposit. A landlord shall not require a security deposit in an amount or value in excess of two months' periodic rent. Uses upon termination. Upon termination of the tenancy, the landlord may apply the security deposit to: (i) Any rent or late fees owed; (ii) Damage caused by the tenant under § 55.1-1227, excluding ordinary wear and tear; (iii) Other damage or charges as provided in the rental agreement; (iv) Actual damages caused by the tenant's breach of the rental agreement (§ 55.1-1251). Return deadline. The landlord shall return the security deposit, less lawful deductions, together with an itemized accounting of any deductions, within 45 days after the termination date or the date the tenant vacates, whichever is later.
B. Multiple tenants. Unless all tenants agree otherwise in writing, the refund shall be payable to all tenants as a single payment.
C. Utilities. A landlord may withhold a portion of the security deposit for third-party utility charges if written notice is provided at least 15 days before deposit processing.
D. Expedited processing. A landlord may offer expedited deposit processing for a fee if the tenant requests it in writing.
E. Deductions during tenancy. If a landlord makes deductions from the security deposit during the tenancy, the landlord must provide a written accounting within 30 days of the deduction. Failure to comply results in court ordering return of the deposit plus actual damages and reasonable attorney's fees.
F. Records. A landlord shall maintain records of all security deposit deductions for two years and make them available for tenant inspection.
G. Move-out inspection. When a landlord requires a tenant to vacate, or within 5 days after receiving the tenant's notice to vacate, the landlord must notify the tenant of the right to be present at the move-out inspection. The inspection must occur within 72 hours of vacating. The landlord must then provide a written itemized list of damages.
H. Damage insurance alternative. A landlord may permit a tenant to provide damage insurance instead of a security deposit, provided the insurer is licensed by the Virginia State Corporation Commission and the policy meets specified requirements.
I. Tenant may convert. A tenant who initially chose damage insurance may convert to a security deposit at any time without the landlord changing the lease terms."}

E. Deductions during tenancy. If a landlord makes deductions from the security deposit during the tenancy, the landlord must provide a written accounting within 30 days of the deduction. Failure to comply results in court ordering return of the deposit plus actual damages and reasonable attorney's fees.
F. Records. A landlord shall maintain records of all security deposit deductions for two years and make them available for tenant inspection.
G. Move-out inspection. When a landlord requires a tenant to vacate, or within 5 days after receiving the tenant's notice to vacate, the landlord must notify the tenant of the right to be present at the move-out inspection. The inspection must occur within 72 hours of vacating. The landlord must then provide a written itemized list of damages.
H. Damage insurance alternative. A landlord may permit a tenant to provide damage insurance instead of a security deposit, provided the insurer is licensed by the Virginia State Corporation Commission and the policy meets specified requirements.
I. Tenant may convert. A tenant who initially chose damage insurance may convert to a security deposit at any time without the landlord changing the lease terms."}

{"statutes":"Va. Code §§ 55.1-1245, 55.1-1245 (two versions: current and effective July 1, 2028)","notice_requirements":{"nonpayment_of_rent":"5-day written notice to pay or vacate (current version); same with extended notice periods (2028 version)","holdover_tenant":"30-day notice to terminate for month-to-month tenancy","lease_violation":"21-day notice to cure for material breach; 30-day notice before termination","no_cause":"30-day notice for month-to-month (VRLTA-covered units)","criminal_activity":"Immediate termination allowed for drug crimes or criminal acts threatening health/safety"},"full_text":"§ 55.1-1245. Noncompliance with rental agreement; monetary penalty.
A. Material breach. If the tenant commits a material breach of the rental agreement or a violation of § 55.1-1227 that materially affects health and safety, the landlord may give written notice specifying the tenant's acts or omissions and stating that the rental agreement will terminate no less than 30 days after receipt of the notice if the breach is not remedied within 21 days.
B. If the breach is remediable by repair or the payment of damages and the tenant adequately remedies the breach within 21 days, the rental agreement does not terminate.
C. Nonremediable breach. If the breach is nonremediable by repair or the payment of damages, the landlord may give written notice stating that the rental agreement will terminate no less than 30 days after receipt.
D. Immediate termination. If the tenant's breach consists of or constitutes a criminal or intentional act that is nonremediable and threatens health or safety, including drug-related criminal activity (as defined under § 54.1-3400), the landlord may terminate immediately and bring an action for possession. Court must hold first hearing within 15 calendar days of service.
E. Repeated breach. If a tenant who has previously received a written notice to remedy a breach and has remedied it commits the same type of breach again, the landlord may give 30 days' written notice to terminate.
F. Nonpayment of rent. If the tenant fails to pay rent when due, the landlord may give written notice requiring the tenant to pay rent or vacate within 5 days. For NSF checks, electronic payment failures, or bad faith stop payments, the tenant must pay within 5 days by cash, certified check, cashier's check, or completed electronic transfer.
J. COVID-19 Rental History Protection (2025). Landlords with more than 4 units may not take adverse action based solely on payment history or eviction records from March 12, 2020 through 30 days after the state of emergency ends. Applicants may dispute within 7 days. Violation carries $1,000 statutory damages plus attorney fees."}

If the tenant commits a material breach of the rental agreement or a violation of § 55.1-1227 that materially affects health and safety, the landlord may give written notice specifying the tenant's acts or omissions and stating that the rental agreement will terminate no less than 30 days after receipt of the notice if the breach is not remedied within 21 days.
B. If the breach is remediable by repair or the payment of damages and the tenant adequately remedies the breach within 21 days, the rental agreement does not terminate.
C. Nonremediable breach. If the breach is nonremediable by repair or the payment of damages, the landlord may give written notice stating that the rental agreement will terminate no less than 30 days after receipt.
D. Immediate termination. If the tenant's breach consists of or constitutes a criminal or intentional act that is nonremediable and threatens health or safety, including drug-related criminal activity (as defined under § 54.1-3400), the landlord may terminate immediately and bring an action for possession. Court must hold first hearing within 15 calendar days of service.
E. Repeated breach. If a tenant who has previously received a written notice to remedy a breach and has remedied it commits the same type of breach again, the landlord may give 30 days' written notice to terminate.
F. Nonpayment of rent. If the tenant fails to pay rent when due, the landlord may give written notice requiring the tenant to pay rent or vacate within 5 days. For NSF checks, electronic payment failures, or bad faith stop payments, the tenant must pay within 5 days by cash, certified check, cashier's check, or completed electronic transfer.
J. COVID-19 Rental History Protection (2025). Landlords with more than 4 units may not take adverse action based solely on payment history or eviction records from March 12, 2020 through 30 days after the state of emergency ends. Applicants may dispute within 7 days. Violation carries $1,000 statutory damages plus attorney fees."}

sources.title:
{"statutes":"Va. Code §§ 55.1-1210, 55.1-1229","full_text":"§ 55.1-1210. Landlord and tenant remedies for abuse of access.
If the tenant refuses to allow lawful access, the landlord may obtain injunctive relief to enforce access, or terminate the rental agreement. In either case, the landlord may recover actual damages and reasonable attorney fees.
If the landlord makes an unlawful entry or makes a lawful entry in an unreasonable manner, or repeatedly demands entry even if lawful, the tenant may obtain injunctive relief, terminate the rental agreement, and recover actual damages and reasonable attorney fees.
§ 55.1-1229. Access; consent; correction of emergency conditions; tenant relocation; security systems.
(Governs tenant's obligation to allow landlord access and landlord's duties when entering; requires tenant not to unreasonably refuse access.)"}

sources.title:
{"statutes":"Va. Code § 55.1-1220","full_text":"§ 55.1-1220. Landlord to maintain fit premises.
A. The landlord shall: (1) Comply with applicable building and housing codes materially affecting health and safety; (2) Make all repairs necessary to keep the premises in a fit and habitable condition; (3) Keep common areas of multi-family premises clean and safe; (4) Maintain in good working order all electrical, plumbing, sanitary, heating, ventilating, air conditioning and other facilities and appliances, including elevators, provided by the landlord; (5) Maintain the premises to prevent accumulation of moisture and mold and remediate mold in accordance with § 8.01-226.12(E); (6) Provide and maintain appropriate garbage containers and arrange for garbage removal; (7) Supply running water and reasonable hot water at all times, and reasonable heating and air conditioning as appropriate for the season; (8) Provide certification that all smoke alarms are present, inspected, and in good working order, no more than once every 12 months.
B. Limitation of liability. The landlord is liable only for actual damages directly resulting from failure to exercise ordinary care.
C. Tenant-assigned duties. Landlord and tenant may agree in writing that the tenant performs certain obligations, provided the agreement is made in good faith and does not diminish the landlord's obligations to other tenants."}

The landlord shall: (1) Comply with applicable building and housing codes materially affecting health and safety; (2) Make all repairs necessary to keep the premises in a fit and habitable condition; (3) Keep common areas of multi-family premises clean and safe; (4) Maintain in good working order all electrical, plumbing, sanitary, heating, ventilating, air conditioning and other facilities and appliances, including elevators, provided by the landlord; (5) Maintain the premises to prevent accumulation of moisture and mold and remediate mold in accordance with § 8.01-226.12(E); (6) Provide and maintain appropriate garbage containers and arrange for garbage removal; (7) Supply running water and reasonable hot water at all times, and reasonable heating and air conditioning as appropriate for the season; (8) Provide certification that all smoke alarms are present, inspected, and in good working order, no more than once every 12 months.
B. Limitation of liability. The landlord is liable only for actual damages directly resulting from failure to exercise ordinary care.
C. Tenant-assigned duties. Landlord and tenant may agree in writing that the tenant performs certain obligations, provided the agreement is made in good faith and does not diminish the landlord's obligations to other tenants."}
